titleStmt.title: Quod qualitates incorporeae sint
titleStmt.funder: Initiative humanités biomédicales de l’Alliance Sorbonne Université

respStmt.resp: Published original versions of the electronic texts. The following text is taken verbatim from Galen’s complete works published by Karl Gottlob Kühn from 1821 to 1830. The text was produced from the image of Kühn’s edition digitised by the Bibliothèque numérique Medica (BIU Santé médecine, Université Paris Cité) using ABBYY FineReader 15.
respStmt.orgName: Galenus verbatim, funded by the Institut universitaire de France (2019-2024), the Initiative humanités biomédicales de l’Alliance Sorbonne Université (2021-2024) and the Institut des sciences de l’Antiquité de l’Alliance Sorbonne Université (2024-2025)

Galenus verbatim, funded by the Institut universitaire de France (2019-2024), the Initiative humanités biomédicales de l’Alliance Sorbonne Université (2021-2024) and the Institut des sciences de l’Antiquité de l’Alliance Sorbonne Université (2024-2025)

Galenus verbatim, funded by the Institut universitaire de France (2019-2024), the Initiative humanités biomédicales de l’Alliance Sorbonne Université (2021-2024) and the Institut des sciences de l’Antiquité de l’Alliance Sorbonne Université (2024-2025)

Galenus verbatim, funded by the Institut universitaire de France (2019-2024), the Initiative humanités biomédicales de l’Alliance Sorbonne Université (2021-2024) and the Institut des sciences de l’Antiquité de l’Alliance Sorbonne Université (2024-2025)

editionStmt.edition: Digital edition of the texts printed in Kühn’s edition. 2024

availability.licence: Available under a Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike 4.0 International License

monogr.title: Γαληνοῦ Ἅπαντα. Galeni Pergameni Opera omnia
monogr.author: Galenus

monogr.title: Magni Hippocratis Coi, et Claudii Galeni Pergameni archiatrΩn uniuersa quae extant
monogr.author: Galenus

encodingDesc.p: The following text is encoded in accordance with EpiDoc standards and with the CTS/CITE Architecture.

correction.p: Typographical errors of the printed edition are marked up by a <choice> element.

hyphenation.p: End-of-line hyphenation is silently removed where appropriate.

interpretation.p: The layout of the printed edition is marked up by <pb> and <lb> elements. The indentation type has been systematically specified with a rend value.
Titles for books of treatises are marked up by a <head> element.
Divisions in works, books, chapters, sections are marked up by a <div> element.
Headings are marked up by a <label> element. Greek numbers in headings of chapters or sections are marked up by a <num> element.
Verse lines are marked up by an <l> element.
Linegroups are marked up by an <lg> element.
Tables are marked up by a <table> element.
In the commentaries, Hippocratic lemmas are marked up by a <quote> element.

normalization.p: Original symbols are maintained as they appear in Kühn’s printed edition. In the Greek text, the following symbols are used: ϛ (stigma: U+03DB), ϟ (koppa: U+03DF), ϡ (sampi: U+03E1), S" (as Galen describes this symbol as a ‘Roman S’ [Ῥωμαϊκὸν S: 19.757.6-7], the Unicode character 𐅵 for ‘half’, U+10175, is not appropriate), 𐆃 (litra: U+10183), 𐅻 (drachma: U+1017B), 𐆈 (gramma: U+10188), 𐅼 (obol: U+1017C), 𐅽 (two obols: U+1017D), Ͻ (capital lunate reverse sigma: U+03FD), 𐆅 (xestes: U+10185), 𐆂ο (kyathos: U+10182+ super omicron 03BF), ♃ (Jupiter: U+2643). In the pseudo-Galenic text Prognostica de decubitu ex mathematica scientia (urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0530.tlg024.verbatim-grc1), the following astrological symbols are also used: ♒ (aquarius: U+2652), ♈ (aries: U+2648), ♋ (cancer: U+264B), ♑ (capricorn: U+2651), ♀ (female sign: U+2640), ♊ (gemini: U+264A), ☾ (last quarter moon: U+263E), ♌ (leo: U+264C), ♎ (libra: U+264E), ♂ (male sign: U+2642), ☿ (mercury: U+263F), ☍ (opposition: U+260D), ♓ (pisces: U+2653), ♐ (sagittarius: U+2650), ♄ (saturn: U+2644), ♏ (scorpio: U+264F), ☉ (sun: U+2609), ♉ (taurus: U+2649), ♍ (virgo: U+264D), □ (white square: U+25A1).
The Unicode character dexia keraia (U+0374) is used to indicate the numeric use of Greek letters.
